```haskell
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
-- Script Execution
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

{- | Execute a Lua script in the sandbox.

This function:
1. Acquires the toolbox lock for thread safety
2. Records start time
3. Loads and executes the script
4. Converts the return value to JSON
5. Returns the result or an error

The script should return a value that can be converted to JSON:
* nil -> null
* boolean -> boolean
* number -> number
* string -> string
* table -> object/array (if table has only integer keys starting at 1, it's an array)
-}
executeScript ::
    Toolbox ->
    Text ->  -- ^ Lua script source
    IO (Either ScriptError ExecutionResult)
executeScript toolbox script =
    -- Without portal - just execute script with no tool access
    executeScriptWithPortal toolbox script Nothing []

{- | Execute a Lua script with access to the tool portal.

This is the full execution function that:
1. Sets up the tool portal as a global Lua function
2. Executes the script with the portal available
3. Handles timeouts and memory limits
4. Provides detailed error information

The portal is exposed to Lua as a 'call_tool' function that takes
a tool name and arguments table.
-}
executeScriptWithPortal ::
    Toolbox ->
    Text ->  -- ^ Lua script source
    Maybe ToolPortal ->  -- ^ Optional tool portal for calling other tools
    [Text] ->  -- ^ Allowed tools whitelist
    IO (Either ScriptError ExecutionResult)
executeScriptWithPortal toolbox script mPortal allowedTools = do
    -- Acquire lock to serialize access within this toolbox instance
    withMVar (toolboxLock toolbox) $ \() -> do
        executeScriptInternal toolbox script mPortal allowedTools

executeScriptInternal ::
    Toolbox ->
    Text ->
    Maybe ToolPortal ->
    [Text] ->
    IO (Either ScriptError ExecutionResult)
executeScriptInternal toolbox script mPortal allowedTools = do
    startTime <- getCurrentTime
    let lstate = toolboxLuaState toolbox
    let maxTime = (toolboxConfig toolbox).luaToolboxMaxExecutionTimeSeconds

    -- Set up portal if provided
    case mPortal of
        Just portal -> setupPortal lstate portal allowedTools
        Nothing -> pure ()

    -- Execute with timeout
    result <- applyTimeout maxTime $ do
        Lua.runWith lstate $ do
            -- Load the script
            status <- Lua.loadbuffer (Text.encodeUtf8 script) "=user"
            if status /= Lua.OK
                then do
                    errMsg <- Lua.tostring' Lua.stackTop
                    Lua.pop 1
                    pure $ Left $ LuaRuntimeError (Text.decodeUtf8 errMsg)
                else do
                    -- Execute the loaded chunk
                    callResult <- pcallWithTraceback 0 Lua.multret
                    if fst callResult /= Lua.OK
                        then do
                            let errMsg = snd callResult
                            pure $ Left $ LuaRuntimeError errMsg
                        else do
                            -- Convert result to JSON
                            nrets <- Lua.gettop
                            jsonValue <- if nrets > 0
                                then luaToJsonValue
                                else pure Aeson.Null
                            Lua.pop nrets
                            pure $ Right jsonValue

    endTime <- getCurrentTime
    let execTime = diffUTCTime endTime startTime

    case result of
        Nothing ->
            -- Timeout occurred
            pure $ Left $ TimeoutError maxTime
        Just (Left err) ->
            pure $ Left err
        Just (Right val) ->
            pure $ Right ExecutionResult
                { resultValue = val
                , resultExecutionTime = execTime
                }

-- | Helper to call Lua function with stack trace capture
pcallWithTraceback :: Int -> Int -> Lua (Lua.Status, Text)
pcallWithTraceback nargs nresults = do
    -- Get debug.traceback for error handling
    Lua.getglobal "debug"
    Lua.getfield Lua.stackTop "traceback"
    Lua.remove (Lua.nthFromTop 2)  -- remove 'debug' table

    -- Insert traceback function before function to be called
    Lua.insert (Lua.nthFromTop (2 + nargs))

    -- Call with error handler
    status <- Lua.pcall nargs nresults (Lua.nthFromTop (-(2 + nargs)))

    if status /= Lua.OK
        then do
            errMsg <- Lua.tostring' Lua.stackTop
            Lua.pop 1
            pure (status, Text.decodeUtf8 errMsg)
        else
            pure (status, "")

-- | Set up the tool portal as a Lua function
setupPortal :: LuaState -> ToolPortal -> [Text] -> IO ()
setupPortal lstate portal allowedTools = do
    -- This will be implemented in Phase 3 when we create the tools module
    -- For now, just create a placeholder
    Lua.runWith lstate $ do
        Lua.pushstring "Tool portal not yet implemented"
        Lua.setglobal "__PORTAL_ERROR"

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
-- Lua to JSON Conversion
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

{- | Convert the value at the top of the Lua stack to JSON.

Handles:
* nil -> Null
* boolean -> Bool
* number -> Number (integer if whole number)
* string -> String
* table -> Object or Array (detected by keys)
-}
luaToJsonValue :: Lua Aeson.Value
luaToJsonValue = do
    ltype <- Lua.ltype Lua.stackTop
    case ltype of
        Lua.TypeNil -> do
            Lua.pop 1
            pure Aeson.Null
        Lua.TypeBoolean -> do
            b <- Lua.toboolean Lua.stackTop
            Lua.pop 1
            pure $ Aeson.Bool b
        Lua.TypeNumber -> do
            n <- Lua.tonumber Lua.stackTop
            Lua.pop 1
            -- Check if it's an integer
            let intVal = round n :: Int
            if fromIntegral intVal == n
                then pure $ Aeson.Number (fromIntegral intVal)
                else pure $ Aeson.Number (fromRational (toRational n))
        Lua.TypeString -> do
            s <- Lua.tostring' Lua.stackTop
            Lua.pop 1
            pure $ Aeson.String (Text.decodeUtf8 s)
        Lua.TypeTable -> do
            convertTable
        _ -> do
            -- For other types (function, userdata, thread, lightuserdata),
            -- convert to string representation
            Lua.pushstring "__tostring"
            Lua.gettable (Lua.nthFromTop 2)
            if Lua.istable (Lua.nthFromTop 2)
                then do
                    -- Call tostring metamethod if exists
                    Lua.insert (Lua.nthFromTop 2)
                    _ <- Lua.pcall 1 1 Nothing
                    s <- Lua.tostring' Lua.stackTop
                    Lua.pop 1
                    pure $ Aeson.String (Text.decodeUtf8 s)
                else do
                    Lua.pop 1  -- pop __tostring
                    s <- Lua.tostring' Lua.stackTop
                    Lua.pop 1
                    pure $ Aeson.String (Text.decodeUtf8 s)

-- | Convert a Lua table to JSON (object or array)
convertTable :: Lua Aeson.Value
convertTable = do
    -- Check if it's an array (integer keys starting at 1, contiguous)
    isArr <- isArray
    if isArr
        then convertArray
        else convertObject

-- | Check if the table at top of stack is an array
isArray :: Lua Bool
isArray = do
    Lua.pushnil  -- first key
    isSequential 1
  where
    isSequential :: Int -> Lua Bool
    isSequential expectedIdx = do
        hasNext <- Lua.next (Lua.nthFromTop 2)
        if not hasNext
            then pure True
            else do
                -- Check if key is the expected integer
                isNum <- Lua.isnumber Lua.stackTop
                if isNum
                    then do
                        idx <- Lua.tointeger Lua.stackTop
                        Lua.pop 2  -- pop key and value
                        if idx == expectedIdx
                            then isSequential (expectedIdx + 1)
                            else pure False
                    else do
                        Lua.pop 2  -- pop key and value
                        pure False

-- | Convert array table to JSON Array
convertArray :: Lua Aeson.Value
convertArray = do
    vals <- collectArrayValues
    pure $ Aeson.Array (Data.Vector.fromList vals)

-- | Collect values from an array table
-- Pop the table when done
collectArrayValues :: Lua [Aeson.Value]
collectArrayValues = do
    let lenIdx = Lua.nthFromTop 1
    Lua.len lenIdx
    len' <- Lua.tointeger Lua.stackTop
    Lua.pop 1

    vals <- mapM (\i -> do
        Lua.pushinteger i
        Lua.gettable (Lua.nthFromTop 2)
        val <- luaToJsonValue  -- This will pop the value
        pure val
        ) [1..len']

    Lua.pop 1  -- pop the table
    pure vals

-- | Convert object table to JSON Object
convertObject :: Lua Aeson.Value
convertObject = do
    -- Pop the table when done
    pairs <- collectObjectPairs
    pure $ Aeson.Object (Data.HashMap.Strict.fromList pairs)

collectObjectPairs :: Lua [(Text, Aeson.Value)]
collectObjectPairs = do
    Lua.pushnil  -- first key
    go []
  where
    go :: [(Text, Aeson.Value)] -> Lua [(Text, Aeson.Value)]
    go acc = do
        hasNext <- Lua.next (Lua.nthFromTop 2)
        if not hasNext
            then do
                Lua.pop 1  -- pop the table
                pure acc
            else do
                -- Stack: table, key, value
                key <- Lua.tostring' (Lua.nthFromTop 2)
                val <- luaToJsonValue  -- This pops the value
                -- Stack: table, key
                Lua.pop 1  -- pop key
                -- Stack: table
                go ((Text.decodeUtf8 key, val) : acc)
```

## Additional Dependencies

Add to `agents.cabal`:

```cabal
  build-depends:    ...existing deps...
                    , hslua >= 2.3
                    , hslua-marshalling
                    , vector
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `System.Agents.Tools.LuaToolbox` module created with all core types
- [ ] `initializeToolbox` creates a sandboxed Lua state
- [ ] `configureSandbox` removes dangerous functions (os.execute, io.popen, etc.)
- [ ] `executeScript` runs Lua code and returns JSON-convertible results
- [ ] Memory limit infrastructure in place (even if approximate)
- [ ] Timeout enforcement works via `applyTimeout`
- [ ] Lua-to-JSON conversion handles nil, boolean, number, string, table types
- [ ] Table detection correctly identifies arrays vs objects
- [ ] Error handling captures stack traces and Lua errors
- [ ] Module compiles without errors
- [ ] Basic tests verify sandbox (os.execute fails) and simple scripts work

## Tradeoffs Documented

1. **Memory tracking**: Using Haskell-level tracking rather than custom Lua allocator for simplicity. May allow slight memory overruns.
2. **Timeout via Haskell async**: Using `timeout` from `System.Timeout` rather than Lua hooks for cleaner implementation.
3. **Table detection**: Arrays detected by checking for sequential integer keys starting at 1 (Lua convention). Non-sequential tables become objects.
4. **Error stack traces**: Using `debug.traceback` for helpful error messages, though this exposes some internal information.

## Next Phase

- #273 (Standard library modules: json, text, http, fs, time)"}

contents.userPrompt:
You are an expert Haskell programmer specialized in analyzing user requirements and Haskell code.
You prepare well-documented individual issues.
You should chunk the work into smaller issues following the 'Feature Branch Isolation' model:
- Identify a 'Feature Branch' name (e.g., 'gh-123' for issue #123).
- All sub-tasks for this feature must use this branch name as their 'branch_name' argument.
- All sub-tasks should pull from and merge to this feature branch.
- Enforce linearization of work by setting 'dependencies' appropriately (e.g., task B depends on task A).
- Add a final task (the 'ultimate PR') that merges the feature branch into 'main'. This task must have 'is_final' set to 'true'.
Each individual issue you create will be executed by a human of software agent independently from each other and in the order specified by dependencies.
Your strategy should be as follows:
- avoid chunks that will generate conflicts (i.e., individual issues should not ask to modify the same file unless they are sequential)
- repeat important data types between different tasks for a same project when they are used in multiple files
You mention important tradeoffs.
You can ask user questions, when doing so always assume the answer could be 'i do not know'.
Do not create tracking issues.
Your output format may be stored as commit message: hence the first line of your last message should be a summary line.

function.arguments:
{"title": "Phase 1: Add LuaToolbox core types and extend ToolExecutionContext with Tool Portal", "dependencies": "", "branch_name": "gh-270", "is_final": "false", "body": "## Summary

Establish the foundation for LuaToolbox by adding core types and extending the tool execution infrastructure to support the inter-toolbox portal mechanism.

## Background

This is Phase 1 of implementing LuaToolbox (#270). We need to:
1. Add `LuaToolboxDescription` to `BuiltinToolboxDescription` in `System.Agents.Base`
2. Extend `ToolExecutionContext` in `System.Agents.Tools.Context` with portal support
3. Define the `ToolPortal` type and related types

## Changes Required

### 1. System.Agents.Base - Add LuaToolboxDescription

Add to `BuiltinToolboxDescription`:

```haskell
-- | Configuration for a Lua builtin toolbox.
--
-- This describes a sandboxed Lua interpreter that can orchestrate other tools
-- through the tool portal mechanism.
--
-- Example configuration:
--
-- @
-- {
--   "tag": "LuaToolbox",
--   "contents": {
--     "name": "lua",
--     "description": "Sandboxed Lua interpreter for tool orchestration",
--     "maxMemoryMB": 256,
--     "maxExecutionTimeSeconds": 300,
--     "allowedTools": ["bash", "sqlite", "io"],
--     "allowedPaths": ["./repro", "./logs"],
--     "allowedHosts": ["localhost", "127.0.0.1"]
--   }
-- }
-- @
data LuaToolboxDescription = LuaToolboxDescription
    { luaToolboxName :: Text
    -- ^ Unique name for this toolbox instance (used as tool prefix)
    , luaToolboxDescription :: Text
    -- ^ Human-readable description of the toolbox purpose
    , luaToolboxMaxMemoryMB :: Int
    -- ^ Maximum Lua heap memory in megabytes
    , luaToolboxMaxExecutionTimeSeconds :: Int
    -- ^ Maximum script execution time in seconds
    , luaToolboxAllowedTools :: [Text]
    -- ^ Whitelist of tool names that Lua scripts can call via the portal
    , luaToolboxAllowedPaths :: [FilePath]
    -- ^ Whitelist of filesystem paths accessible to Lua scripts
    , luaToolboxAllowedHosts :: [Text]
    -- ^ Whitelist of network hosts accessible to Lua HTTP module
    }
    deriving (Show, Ord, Eq, Generic)

-- Add to BuiltinToolboxDescription:
data BuiltinToolboxDescription
    = SqliteToolbox SqliteToolboxDescription
    | SystemToolbox SystemToolboxDescription
    | LuaToolbox LuaToolboxDescription  -- NEW
    deriving (Show, Ord, Eq, Generic)

-- Add JSON instances (tag-based serialization like other toolboxes)
```

### 2. System.Agents.Tools.Context - Add Tool Portal Support

Add new types:

```haskell
import Data.Time (NominalDiffTime)

-- | Unique identifier for tool invocation traces
type TraceId = Text

-- | A tool call from Lua (or another tool) to another tool through the portal
--
-- This represents a synchronous call where the caller waits for the result.
data ToolCall = ToolCall
    { callToolName :: Text
    -- ^ The name of the tool to invoke (e.g., "bash", "sqlite_query")
    , callArgs :: Aeson.Value
    -- ^ JSON arguments for the tool
    , callCallerId :: Text
    -- ^ Identifier for the calling tool (for tracing: who called whom)
    }
    deriving (Show, Eq, Generic)

instance ToJSON ToolCall
instance FromJSON ToolCall

-- | Result of a tool invocation through the portal
data ToolResult = ToolResult
    { resultData :: Aeson.Value
    -- ^ The result data from the tool
    , resultDuration :: NominalDiffTime
    -- ^ How long the invocation took
    , resultTraceId :: TraceId
    -- ^ Trace ID for book-keeping and call tree visualization
    }
    deriving (Show, Eq, Generic)

instance ToJSON ToolResult
instance FromJSON ToolResult

-- | The tool portal: a callback function that allows tools to invoke other tools
--
-- This is the core inter-toolbox communication mechanism. When Lua (or another
-- tool) wants to call another tool, it uses this portal.
type ToolPortal = ToolCall -> IO ToolResult
```

Extend `ToolExecutionContext`:

```haskell
data ToolExecutionContext = ToolExecutionContext
    { ctxSessionId :: SessionId
    , ctxConversationId :: ConversationId
    , ctxTurnId :: TurnId
    , ctxAgentId :: Maybe AgentId
    , ctxFullSession :: Maybe Session
    , ctxCallStack :: [CallStackEntry]
    , ctxMaxDepth :: Maybe Int
    , ctxToolPortal :: Maybe ToolPortal  -- NEW: inter-toolbox portal
    , ctxAllowedTools :: [Text]          -- NEW: whitelist for security
    }
    deriving (Show, Eq, Generic)
```

Add constructor helpers:

```haskell
-- | Create a context with tool portal support
mkPortalContext ::
    SessionId ->
    ConversationId ->
    TurnId ->
    Maybe AgentId ->
    Maybe Session ->
    [CallStackEntry] ->
    Maybe Int ->
    Maybe ToolPortal ->
    [Text] ->  -- allowed tools
    ToolExecutionContext
mkPortalContext sessId convId tId mAgentId mSession stack maxDepth portal allowed =
    ToolExecutionContext
        { ctxSessionId = sessId
        , ctxConversationId = convId
        , ctxTurnId = tId
        , ctxAgentId = mAgentId
        , ctxFullSession = mSession
        , ctxCallStack = stack
        , ctxMaxDepth = maxDepth
        , ctxToolPortal = portal
        , ctxAllowedTools = allowed
        }

-- | Check if a tool is allowed in this context
isToolAllowed :: Text -> ToolExecutionContext -> Bool
isToolAllowed toolName ctx =
    -- Empty allowed list means all tools allowed (backward compatibility)
    null (ctxAllowedTools ctx) || toolName `elem` ctxAllowedTools ctx
```

Update existing constructors to maintain backward compatibility:

```haskell
mkToolExecutionContext :: ... -> ToolExecutionContext
mkToolExecutionContext sessId convId tId mAgentId mSession stack maxDepth =
    ToolExecutionContext
        { ...
        , ctxToolPortal = Nothing
        , ctxAllowedTools = []
        }

mkMinimalContext :: ... -> ToolExecutionContext
mkMinimalContext sessId convId tId =
    ToolExecutionContext
        { ...
        , ctxToolPortal = Nothing
        , ctxAllowedTools = []
        }

mkRootContext :: ... -> ToolExecutionContext  
mkRootContext sessId convId tId mAgentId mSession maxDepth =
    ToolExecutionContext
        { ...
        , ctxToolPortal = Nothing
        , ctxAllowedTools = []
        }
```

### 3. System.Agents.Tools.Base - Add LuaTool to ToolDef

```haskell
data ToolDef
    = BashTool !BashTools.ScriptDescription
    | MCPTool !McpTools.ToolDescription
    | IOTool !IOTools.IOScriptDescription
    | OpenAPITool !Text !Text
    | PostgRESTool !Text !Text
    | SqliteTool !SqliteTools.ToolDescription
    | SystemTool !SystemTools.ToolDescription
    | LuaTool !Text  -- NEW: toolbox name, Lua scripts are anonymous
    deriving (Show)
```

And add corresponding `CallResult` constructor:

```haskell
data CallResult call
    = ...existing constructors...
    | LuaToolResult call Aeson.Value  -- NEW
    | LuaToolError call Text          -- NEW
    deriving (Show)
```

Update `mapCallResult` and `extractCall` to handle new constructors.

### 4. System.Agents.Tools.Trace - Add LuaToolbox trace

```haskell
data ToolTrace
    = BashToolsTrace !BashTools.RunTrace
    | IOToolsTrace (IOTools.Trace Aeson.Value ByteString)
    | SqliteToolsTrace !SqliteTools.Trace
    | SystemToolsTrace !SystemTools.Trace
    | LuaToolsTrace !Text  -- NEW: trace message or event type
    deriving (Show)
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `LuaToolboxDescription` added to `BuiltinToolboxDescription` with proper JSON instances
- [ ] `ToolCall`, `ToolResult`, `ToolPortal`, `TraceId` types defined in `System.Agents.Tools.Context`
- [ ] `ToolExecutionContext` extended with `ctxToolPortal` and `ctxAllowedTools`
- [ ] `ToolDef` extended with `LuaTool`
- [ ] `CallResult` extended with `LuaToolResult` and `LuaToolError`
- [ ] `ToolTrace` extended with `LuaToolsTrace`
- [ ] Constructor helpers maintain backward compatibility
- [ ] JSON serialization/deserialization works correctly for new types
- [ ] Code compiles without errors

## Tradeoffs Documented

1. **Synchronous portal**: The `ToolPortal` is synchronous (`IO ToolResult`) for simplicity. Async support can be added later if needed.
2. **Empty allowed list = all allowed**: For backward compatibility, an empty `ctxAllowedTools` list means all tools are allowed. This is safe because existing code won't set the portal anyway.
3. **TraceId as Text**: Using `Text` for `TraceId` rather than a newtype for simplicity. Can be strengthened later.

## Dependencies

None - this is the foundational issue for the LuaToolbox feature.

## Related

- Parent feature: #270
- Next phase: #271 (Core Lua interpreter)"}

message.content:
Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the project structure. Let me create a series of GitHub issues that break down this feature into manageable chunks following the Feature Branch Isolation model.

The key files to understand:
- `System.Agents.Base` - Contains `BuiltinToolboxDescription`, `Agent` types
- `System.Agents.Tools.Context` - Contains `ToolExecutionContext`
- `System.Agents.Tools.Base` - Contains `Tool`, `ToolDef`, `CallResult`
- `System.Agents.ToolRegistration` - Contains registration logic for toolboxes
- `System.Agents.Tools.SqliteToolbox` - Good reference for implementing a toolbox

I'll create issues that:
1. Are independent where possible (avoid conflicts)
2. Follow the feature branch pattern (`gh-270`)
3. Have clear dependencies
4. Repeat necessary type definitions for independent work

5. Create standard library modules (tools, json, text, http, fs, time, sql)
6. Add security enforcement (whitelist, sandbox)
7. Add tracing for nested tool calls
8. Update ToolRegistration for LuaToolbox
9. Add tests and documentation

Let me create the issues following the Feature Branch Isolation model. The feature branch name should be something like `gh-XYZ` where XYZ will be the issue number. But since we don't know the issue numbers yet, I'll need to be strategic about this.

Actually, I can use placeholder branch names that the human/software agent can adjust when creating the PRs. Or better yet, since I'm creating issues, I can reference them as dependencies.

2. I should use `gh-270` as the feature branch
3. All sub-tasks will branch from gh-270 and merge back to gh-270
4. The final task merges gh-270 to main

Let me create these issues.

```haskell
instance ToJSON ToolCall
instance FromJSON ToolCall

-- | Result of a tool invocation through the portal
data ToolResult = ToolResult
    { resultData :: Aeson.Value
    -- ^ The result data from the tool
    , resultDuration :: NominalDiffTime
    -- ^ How long the invocation took
    , resultTraceId :: TraceId
    -- ^ Trace ID for book-keeping and call tree visualization
    }
    deriving (Show, Eq, Generic)

instance ToJSON ToolResult
instance FromJSON ToolResult

-- | The tool portal: a callback function that allows tools to invoke other tools
--
-- This is the core inter-toolbox communication mechanism. When Lua (or another
-- tool) wants to call another tool, it uses this portal.
type ToolPortal = ToolCall -> IO ToolResult
```

Extend `ToolExecutionContext`:

```haskell
data ToolExecutionContext = ToolExecutionContext
    { ctxSessionId :: SessionId
    , ctxConversationId :: ConversationId
    , ctxTurnId :: TurnId
    , ctxAgentId :: Maybe AgentId
    , ctxFullSession :: Maybe Session
    , ctxCallStack :: [CallStackEntry]
    , ctxMaxDepth :: Maybe Int
    , ctxToolPortal :: Maybe ToolPortal  -- NEW: inter-toolbox portal
    , ctxAllowedTools :: [Text]          -- NEW: whitelist for security
    }
    deriving (Show, Eq, Generic)
```

Add constructor helpers:

```haskell
-- | Create a context with tool portal support
mkPortalContext ::
    SessionId ->
    ConversationId ->
    TurnId ->
    Maybe AgentId ->
    Maybe Session ->
    [CallStackEntry] ->
    Maybe Int ->
    Maybe ToolPortal ->
    [Text] ->  -- allowed tools
    ToolExecutionContext
mkPortalContext sessId convId tId mAgentId mSession stack maxDepth portal allowed =
    ToolExecutionContext
        { ctxSessionId = sessId
        , ctxConversationId = convId
        , ctxTurnId = tId
        , ctxAgentId = mAgentId
        , ctxFullSession = mSession
        , ctxCallStack = stack
        , ctxMaxDepth = maxDepth
        , ctxToolPortal = portal
        , ctxAllowedTools = allowed
        }

-- | Check if a tool is allowed in this context
isToolAllowed :: Text -> ToolExecutionContext -> Bool
isToolAllowed toolName ctx =
    -- Empty allowed list means all tools allowed (backward compatibility)
    null (ctxAllowedTools ctx) || toolName `elem` ctxAllowedTools ctx
```

Update existing constructors to maintain backward compatibility:

```haskell
mkToolExecutionContext :: ... -> ToolExecutionContext
mkToolExecutionContext sessId convId tId mAgentId mSession stack maxDepth =
    ToolExecutionContext
        { ...
        , ctxToolPortal = Nothing
        , ctxAllowedTools = []
        }

mkMinimalContext :: ... -> ToolExecutionContext
mkMinimalContext sessId convId tId =
    ToolExecutionContext
        { ...
        , ctxToolPortal = Nothing
        , ctxAllowedTools = []
        }

mkRootContext :: ... -> ToolExecutionContext  
mkRootContext sessId convId tId mAgentId mSession maxDepth =
    ToolExecutionContext
        { ...
        , ctxToolPortal = Nothing
        , ctxAllowedTools = []
        }
```

### 3. System.Agents.Tools.Base - Add LuaTool to ToolDef

```haskell
data ToolDef
    = BashTool !BashTools.ScriptDescription
    | MCPTool !McpTools.ToolDescription
    | IOTool !IOTools.IOScriptDescription
    | OpenAPITool !Text !Text
    | PostgRESTool !Text !Text
    | SqliteTool !SqliteTools.ToolDescription
    | SystemTool !SystemTools.ToolDescription
    | LuaTool !Text  -- NEW: toolbox name, Lua scripts are anonymous
    deriving (Show)
```

And add corresponding `CallResult` constructor:

```haskell
data CallResult call
    = ...existing constructors...
    | LuaToolResult call Aeson.Value  -- NEW
    | LuaToolError call Text          -- NEW
    deriving (Show)
```

Update `mapCallResult` and `extractCall` to handle new constructors.

### 4. System.Agents.Tools.Trace - Add LuaToolbox trace

```haskell
data ToolTrace
    = BashToolsTrace !BashTools.RunTrace
    | IOToolsTrace (IOTools.Trace Aeson.Value ByteString)
    | SqliteToolsTrace !SqliteTools.Trace
    | SystemToolsTrace !SystemTools.Trace
    | LuaToolsTrace !Text  -- NEW: trace message or event type
    deriving (Show)
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `LuaToolboxDescription` added to `BuiltinToolboxDescription` with proper JSON instances
- [ ] `ToolCall`, `ToolResult`, `ToolPortal`, `TraceId` types defined in `System.Agents.Tools.Context`
- [ ] `ToolExecutionContext` extended with `ctxToolPortal` and `ctxAllowedTools`
- [ ] `ToolDef` extended with `LuaTool`
- [ ] `CallResult` extended with `LuaToolResult` and `LuaToolError`
- [ ] `ToolTrace` extended with `LuaToolsTrace`
- [ ] Constructor helpers maintain backward compatibility
- [ ] JSON serialization/deserialization works correctly for new types
- [ ] Code compiles without errors

## Tradeoffs Documented

1. **Synchronous portal**: The `ToolPortal` is synchronous (`IO ToolResult`) for simplicity. Async support can be added later if needed.
2. **Empty allowed list = all allowed**: For backward compatibility, an empty `ctxAllowedTools` list means all tools are allowed. This is safe because existing code won't set the portal anyway.
3. **TraceId as Text**: Using `Text` for `TraceId` rather than a newtype for simplicity. Can be strengthened later.

## Dependencies

None - this is the foundational issue for the LuaToolbox feature.

## Related

- Parent feature: #270
- Next phase: #271 (Core Lua interpreter)"}

```haskell
-- | Configuration for a Lua builtin toolbox.
```

module System.Agents.Tools.Trace where

{-# LANGUAGE DeriveGeneric #-}
{-# LANGUAGE GeneralizedNewtypeDeriving #-}

module System.Agents.ToolSchema where

import Data.Aeson (FromJSON, ToJSON, Value (..), (.=))
import qualified Data.Aeson as Aeson
import qualified Data.HashMap.Strict as HashMap
import Data.Text (Text)
import GHC.Generics (Generic)

data ParamProperty = ParamProperty
    { propertyKey :: Text
    , propertyType :: ParamType
    , propertyDescription :: Text
    , propertyRequired :: Bool
    {- ^ Whether this property is required. When True, the property key
    is included in the 'required' array of the JSON schema.
    This allows the LLM to omit optional parameters.
    -}
    }
    deriving (Show, Ord, Eq, Generic)
instance FromJSON ParamProperty
instance ToJSON ParamProperty

data ParamType
    = NullParamType
    | StringParamType
    | BoolParamType
    | NumberParamType
    | EnumParamType [Text]
    | OpaqueParamType Text
    | MultipleParamType Text -- todo: break limitation preventing string-enum and null
    | ObjectParamType [ParamProperty]
    deriving (Show, Ord, Eq, Generic)
instance FromJSON ParamType
instance ToJSON ParamType

toJsonSchemaPair :: ParamProperty -> (Text, Value)
toJsonSchemaPair p = (p.propertyKey, Aeson.object (jsonSchema p))

jsonSchema :: ParamProperty -> [(Aeson.Key, Value)]
jsonSchema p =
    case p.propertyType of
        NullParamType ->
            ["type" .= ("null" :: Text), "description" .= p.propertyDescription]
        StringParamType ->
            ["type" .= ("string" :: Text), "description" .= p.propertyDescription]
        BoolParamType ->
            ["type" .= ("boolean" :: Text), "description" .= p.propertyDescription]
        NumberParamType ->
            ["type" .= ("number" :: Text), "description" .= p.propertyDescription]
        EnumParamType allowedValues ->
            ["type" .= ("string" :: Text), "enum" .= allowedValues, "description" .= p.propertyDescription]
        OpaqueParamType typ ->
            ["type" .= typ, "description" .= p.propertyDescription]
        MultipleParamType allowedTypes ->
            ["type" .= allowedTypes, "description" .= p.propertyDescription]
        ObjectParamType propz ->
            let requiredProps = filter propertyRequired propz
             in [ "type" .= ("object" :: Text)
                , "description" .= p.propertyDescription
                , "properties" .= HashMap.fromList (fmap toJsonSchemaPair propz)
                , "additionalProperties" .= False
                , "required" .= fmap propertyKey requiredProps
                ]
